summary: Riccomini argues that analytics engineers and data engineers should be merged back into a single role, reversing the split that occurred over the past decade. He contends that the analytics engineer role, focused primarily on transformations, doesn't provide enough value to justify dedicated headcount. Several factors drove the original bifurcation: the shift to ELT architectures, Kafka adoption, shift-left philosophy, and vendors creating roles to sell products. Now that ZIRP has ended, data pipelines are commoditized, and LLMs can answer many data questions, the conditions that justified separate roles have changed. Tools like dltHub and tabsdata are emerging to facilitate this reunification.
key_points: The analytics engineer role is too limited in scope and too far removed from revenue generation to justify full headcount
The ETL-to-ELT shift and data lake adoption enabled data engineers to ignore transformations while giving analytics engineers something to do
ZIRP ending forced CFOs to scrutinize the cost of analyst and data teams
Data pipeline work is now commoditized with many vendors available to handle it
LLMs and data chatbots are increasingly replacing analysts for answering data questions
money_quotes: I've long held that creating the 'analytics engineer' role was a mistake.
I don't believe that this set of activities is enough value to justify a full headcount; it's too limited in scope and too far removed from revenue generation.
Dumping garbage into an object store made it easy for data engineers to ignore transformations and gave analytics engineers something to do.
dbtLabs, Motherduck, and other MDS vendors were all too willing to create a new role to sell their products, which dovetailed nicely with analyst's desire to be engineers and get paid more.
LLMs are replacing analysts in some cases. Screech all you want, but it's happening.
It's time to merge data engineers and analytics engineers back into a single data team that's responsible for E, T, and L.
I expect to see a collapse of data engineering and analytics engineering back to a single team in the next few years.
key_insight: The economic and technological conditions that justified splitting data warehouse teams into data engineers and analytics engineers have reversed, making reunification both inevitable and necessary.
